I can melt metal i can lol

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Thanks a lot, Robert, for reminding me of my 25 years old arc welder looming around somewhere! 😀👍
I also decided for a more powerful model, being 160 A back than. Struggling with thinner metal sheets, also, but with my power line as well. The breaker constantly flew off, until I discovered the existance of such device called inrush current limiter, relieving me from that nuisance.
Welding is sooo useful. 😍
25 years of occasional welding (I'm more of the glueing guy) I'm still pretty bad in terms of accuracy or shinynes. But why, I've got my angle grinder, removing anything "not so shiny". 😅

BTW: In your chess set video I desparately tried to identify the product originally stored in the card box you reused for the spray-glueing. Now I know, LOL. 😂

author: @Moist_yet_Crispy

author: @RR-mt2wp
text:
The welder is 300amps for the size wow. Im a poor welder dont do enough but As long as it penatrates in to the metal enough.
It looked like a 2mm rod for lighter metal you can use 1.6mm and drop ampage. As you know Migs tig better for sheet metal. Some times a rod sticks you wiggle it but it still sticks it wiĺl glow red as its the thin part of the circuit. I turn off machine then free the rod doesent happen to often but can. Heating tip on a scrap bit off metal your right saves bloabs on your work and vice.  I was always told The welding sparks can fly every were so clean area to work is a must a spark smouldering in a rag etc you wont want.
Your having fun enjoying your new skill. And you will greatly use it.
All my best Ron.

author: @ChopShopGarage
text: Welding thin sheet metal with mma (stick) is possible. The worst part is not putting too much heat on the part and getting warps on the metal like you had. Also the 45 degree is the hardest weld to do when you are beginning to weld. Try small tack welds spread apart and give time to cool down, or cool it down with the air compressor gun (one tack, stop, blow, move to next tack a couple inches away and so on till you reach the end of the plate and then go back to the start in between the first tack welds). Hope this helps a little.

author: @your_utube
text: Fear, excitement...what's really the difference hey? I think safety info is 100 times more important so you do not shock, burn yourself, burn down your workshop or do something that is dangerous. And that is necessary before striking that first arc. Watching someone is the absolute fast track, because you get to ask the WHY questions such as "why is he wearing a helmet?", "why the gloves", "why..." and you get the point. Mig, tig, stick, gas ??? That is less important and only matters after strike 1... I myself have only really done stick. I can see how fearful the first time gas welding might be and I thank you Prof Rob for that reminder. Maybe one day, hey?

author: @FarmerBoBonYoutube
text: If that is a 2.5mm rod 70A might be too low, I will push it to 90-100A for that, when i weld thick steel like 6-8mm with 2,5 rod I normally find it doing well at around 110A... it also depends on the thickness of the steel you want to weld, as welding is actually about melting 2 pieces of steel together by using electrical heat (the arc) but yes I am sure there are much cleverer guys out there and tons of helpful videos :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @FarmerBoBonYoutube
text: Hi Rob, tip for that start up problem... i have been welding farm 30 plus years and I tell you to start easy without striking a lot is kind of nice.. so what I do is just have an ordinary file handy, and every time you want to start welding, just grab the rod close to the end (obviously with your glove on in case it is hot) and give it just one file on the tip of the rod, it clears the tip of flux and carbon buildup etc... then start welding and you will be amazed :)   I promise... if you stop a weld for 10 seconds or less it wouldnt need this, you should be able to start again without a problem.. also make sure your current is not too low for the size rod....  :)

author: @MrBunkerstudio
text: Interesting that you admit fear! It's fear of the "unknown" so you will feel good when it becomes , known! I started as apprentice , in paper mills, so I showed up ! There was always Scottish engineers for some reason! I stood and whitened a massive r,s,J hung at 10 feet, on chain and crane , so up the ladder he went, I had my own ladder, I didn't want to clime up it!! But did, he did 1 line of arkweld about 1 inch long, he said follow me,  he proceeded to walk along the r,s,j to the unwellded end, I felt sick but followed, it was 3 feet wide, then another 1 inch wide weld, he said go back, he put a thumbs up to the crane driver who lowered the chain's, unhook he shouted, so I did, at first I hated him, but after that , I had instent respect!! I thought I would share with you !

author: @aspudkicker
text: Fear is the body's survival mech. Knowledge lets you manage fear so that you can achieve. A piece of knowledge from my own recent experience, I set up a load of steel to weld, put all my ppe on and cracked on. What I didnt realise was even though I had my gauntlets on I had my sleeves rolled up. The arc light burnt my skin like really bad sunburn in the gap between my overall sleeve and gauntlet. I taught myself to weld years ago, the settings on the welders vary between similar welders, the best thing to do is prepare off-cuts of that you are about to weld, and try different settings to see which is best for that particular situation.

author: @datman220001
text: I wish someone had told me this when I started welding many years ago, excellent advice for beginners. The only thing I would say,  you should mention is, weld where there is good ventilation, outside if you can or use an extractor, even something as simple as a fan blowing on your work, if it takes the smoke away from your face, will help greatly. What a lot of people don't realise is the smoke that comes off from a welding arc carries minute particulate of metal which if inhaled can do great damage to your lungs, So don't forget use a fan to blow the smoke away from your face, at the very least.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @Kiyarose3999
text: Im a Sheet Metal Worker/Panel Beater by trade. With the the 20 gauge sheet metal ( the thin plate) really you need a Mig welder or Oxy Acetylene,  striking the rod is common practice among welders. So that was right, although experienced Arc welders learn to strike the rod as they begin to weld. It is a matter of getting the setting right for the right size rod, slightly too low and it makes it harder to start the Arc, and the rod ‘sticks’ to the metal.

When i got back home I purchased a wire feed welder with out the gas envelope, an inert gas tank could be added later. When I started using it I wondered why I wasn't getting a bead, I forgot to clean the piece of steel angle. most steel you buy in the large chain hardware stores is coated with an anti rust film. Welding is a constant learning situation.

author: @edkoetsier339
text: Well Rob, You're getting into all my hobbies. First wind, wind, now welding. Bravo. I find welding thin stuff with arc is a doomed to failure. You blow holes. Mig is better, and Tig is better still. But it can be done with Arc. I know a car restorer who arc welds door skins with arc. Most people (me included) would say that that is the domain of Tig. You need real skill with arc and Tig. Mig is easy. You will be able to arc weld 1mm mild steel, but it might take a lot of practice..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @robertpitt8418
text: Hi ROB: You should practice without holding the rod itself with your other hand  its not something your mind should get used to doing. remember you control the flow of weld, the slower your speed the thicker the weld . Try feeding and gently pushing the rod in a DOME shape motion starting  from left to right on a flat practice piece,    then gradually decrease this DOME in size as you progress and move along,   this should be your simple welding technique for the future, now try welding two pieces together. the structure and neatness  of the weld will depend on your speed and the given motion shape.   after this exercise you should have no problem using one hand and your welding can only improve .    ( welding heavy and tin light gage material together - Always start and remain on the  heaviest gage and let it simply flow.   :  )

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tips mate well appreciated cheers

author: @RB-yq7qv
text:
Robert I would consider purchasing THE CIG WELDING BOOK this will give you a guide to amps to Rod thickness this will assist you. 
For sheet metal you may have been better served purchasing a MIG or TIG welder. For tube welding TIG is what you need. 
A TIG weld to 200 to 220 amps would be ok but you must check the duty cycle as the higher the amps the shorter the time can be spent welding. The better quantity brands is the way to go, and because you would like to do plate and tube a D/D pulse AC / DC Tig will allow you to do Aluminum, Stainless Steel, Brass and mild steel. Tig machines easy to use but I would recommend going to Tafe to learn why procedures are performed before welding anything, remember and good weld you can rely on a bag weld that looks ok is a killer. 
Reading is great but welding is more the reading some lines there are real issues if not performed correctly.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good stuff mate - cheers

author: @AtlasReburdened
text:
When it comes to the really thinner stuff, you can always drop back to 3/32" rods. The heat it takes to get larger rods to melt will blow through the thinner gauge sheet in no time. Also, you really only need great technique if you're doing structural welding. For hobby grade stuff that no lives will depend on, a metal bond is a metal bond.
Oh, and since I've never mentioned it and it's worth knowing whether or not I'm full of it, I've welded everything from competition barbecues to ranch gates to the steel superstructures of dozens of local buildings as a cert'd Ironworker(worst job ever in the Houston summer, btw).

author: @ColinWatters
text: 12:20 I learnt to weld fixing my mower deck which is 2mm steel. Not an ideal place to learn. Very easy to blow holes especially where its rusted thinner. I found the best way to fill a small blow hole is to put the end of a second rod in the hole as a filler. Remove the flux from it first. Do short tack welds striking your arc on the second rod. I saw this done first on a YouTube vid but can't find it now. YouTube videos recommend 1/16th 6013 rods for thin sheet.

author: @tinkmarshino
text: Well done Rob.. First steps can be a bit intimidating....You should point out that there is more to welding then just making a bead if you are talking about structural welds. Critical welds that are going to be under stress need to be done in the correct way and for that you must be taught by a professional . You do not want to weld up a frame on a motor bike for instance just to have the weld break at a most dangerous time. I am sure you are aware of this but you should tell those  folks that you explained welding to in this video so they do not try their welds on critical parts and put there health and safety at risk.. But for non critical welds as long as you have the temp and penetration good enough it should work well...... carry on!

author: @MrAnderson4509
text: you engage in a diversity of materials, I would suggest leaning the TIG process adding it to your shop, aluminium, stainless steel, thin sheet stock, And fairly heavy stuff can I I'll be done with that process not to mention adding to the control and quality of the welding, The first process I learned was oxygen acetylene welding, and I liked the control, taking offers a similar control but with a much higher quality weld, most likely you've already discussed this with your Welding friends, and it is only my opinion and suggestion, but please continue your pursuit, it will be very rewarding. Thank you for all you do.

If it were easy everyone would be doing it!

Having said that ..... Stick, MIG or TIG?  Each has their strengths and weaknesses.
Stick is cheap and handy, but not good for thin sheet!
MIG is middle of the road £ wise, takes a little learning and is good for thin and medium!
TIG is the Rolls Royce of welding! You can do the best welds with TIG (if you are not satisfied with your run you can simply go over it without a filler rod and just re-melt the weld until you are happy!
One aspect of stick welders you missed on is Duty Cycle ..... The shorter the Duty Cycle the lower the time you can weld for and the longer you need to let the welder cool down!

One final point ..... You don't see many old stick welders! The reason is the smoke the flux gives off is Toxic, and whilst you are concentrating on your arc it is easy to be unaware of the smoke drifting under your hood!
Take my advice for what it is worth ..... 30 years ago I learned to weld
.... with Gas! ;0)

replies.author: @michaelpearson5465
replies.text:
You are correct about the fumes that come from the flux burning, but welding with has is no different. I think it was only last year that the fumes from all welding procedures were classed as casonagenic. You have to remember that its not only the flux giving harmful fumes off its also the metal itself. Stainless steel is particularly bad, I have a full extraction system and an air fed mask as I weld it all day every day with duel shield MIG and TIG.

However, for a hobbyist who is only going to pick it up once in a while, they will be absolutely fine with adequate ventilation.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
@michaelpearson5465 You are right ALL fumes are bad!
But a good blast of Oxy can cure a hangover in no time! .... Don't ask how I know that ;0)

author: @phillean
text: Good stuff on the initial fears of welding and the use of self darkening helmets. I know you were staying away from the technical, but a couple set up tips for making it easier in the beginning. Choose soft start rods, it will be written on the packet, they start much more easily. Choose rod thickness to match the job, thick rod, thick metal, more amps. Thin rod, thin metal, fewer amps. Give yourself room for both of your hands to move, particularly so that you can move the rod holding hand smoothly. If you are going to use a starting block (waste meta) put the block close to (in line with) the the intended weld, also clean the rust off your start block, rust makes it harder to start and contaminates the weld. I can imagine you doing lots of close up hot welding so please button up your overalls. Tshirts will only partly stop a UV burn on thin chest skin.

author: @mindaugasvaskevicius1818
text: Don't do the same mistakes I did when learning to weld: don't try to weld thin stuff with stick welding. Don't try to weld materials thinner than the electrodes you are using. For me, I don't weld thinner than 3mm any more. I've learned my lesson.

replies.author: @ColinWatters
replies.text: Well I'm a total newbie and after a month I'm able to weld 1.6 an 2mm sheet with an AC stick welder. Yes it was a nightmare when I first started but you can do it with practice. I've even learnt how to fill blow holes using a second rod as a filler. The real difficult stuff is metal that started out as 1.6mm and has rusted thinner.

author: @alext8828

Back in high school, I found a piece of what I would swear was aluminum and it welded very well with the appropriate rod.  Something for you to try if you care to.

author: @marcogallazzi9049
text: Try and error, watch your welds after you´ve done a couple of straight beats, practice, practice, practice is the only way to learn how to weld. After each weld you´ll have some slag at the end of the rod that needs to be cleaned each time. As long as your not standing on a puddle of water it´s pretty hard to get electrocuted by this :-) Arc welding is hard on thin metal as you said, not impossible though. Maybe a Mig welder with 1mm fuxcore wire would be easier fot that.

author: @garyhardman8369
text:
You might have been better buying a MIG if you intend welding thin material.
The one piece of advice  that I would urge you to heed, is to avoid welding galvanized steel. It's very tempting to re-cycle galvanized sheet for projects.
However, the fumes liberated by welding galvanized steel can be very bad for your lungs. Many years ago, I patched-up the floor of my old car with offcuts of galvanized sheet. For the following couple of weeks, my lungs felt like I had been inhaling acid fumes!
It was many years later, that I discovered that this could have resulted in permanent lung damage.
Love the videos. Stay safe mate!

author: @user-dl4oo9qz2d
text:
If you hold the rod more vertical the center of the rod will conduct.
You tap the rod on the workpiece.

Dry new Rods work the best so store correctly.

Spent a month during my apenticeship full time in a group of 60 guys.
I liked it so got in the top 5 out of 60.
We learnt from the ITC basic welding a4 booklet.

Speaclisled in machining so never took it further, however I recently tried battery welding with four batteries without success on 1mm steel as didn't have the current as was off grid.

replies.author: @phillean
replies.text: For thin tube welding such as bicycle tubing a TIG welding is very useful, it gives fine control without burning holes in the thin metal (after some practice). It is a bit like using an airbrush that has a small flame, compared to the fire stick of an arc welder. TIG welders and TIG supplies are relative cheap these days.

replies.author: @ericvonessex9353
replies.text: @phillean Have to admit my failed attempts at welding in the early 1980's still make me apprehensive but I will certainly look into your valuable advice which is very much appreciated. Building a bike without welding is near impossible. A welder is therefore necessary; a TIG welder sounds perfect as almost all my welding jobs will involve tubing. You have answered my problem, many thanks.

replies.author: @phillean
replies.text: @ericvonessex9353 Modern inverter welders have come a long way and make welding a lot easier than the early home welders. There are lots of TIG welding videos on YouTube that will help you learn. Watch a few before you buy anything.

replies.author: @ericvonessex9353

Your advice has certainly built my confidence and I will take Robert's advice regarding modern Welder's Helmets!  The visor supplied with my old welder, it was impossible to see anything through it! You are right, things have come a long way since the 1980's!

author: @pulesjet
text: Good think to know about arc welding is the function produces BUTT LOADS OF UV LIGHT.  Without proper protection you will get SUN BURNED.

author: @ralphsammis7330
text: Thanks for sharing.  I’m beginning too.  I used a 6000 lumin torch so I could see more.  I saw retinal image afterward so I darkened helmet adjustment.

author: @pulesjet
text: Four turns of 8AWG wire on a Microwave transformer makes fine spot welder.  I learned I can weld quite fine using 3/16 rod and two car batteries in series.  Four 38120 liFePo4 cells works for small jobs.  If your using batteries using Pos Ground prevents spatter.  Current flows from rod to your work.

author: @michaelpearson5465
text:
Striking the electrode like a match is the taught method of initiating an arc, some people think you need to tap it but this leads to the electrode "sticking" to the base metal.

Once you get a feel for how best to start the arc have a go scratching it on the piece you intend to weld, in the middle of the two parent metals you intend to weld.

I remember the first time I picked up a welding torch, it was terrifying for me, as I knew nothing about it. Once you get a feel for what your doing and how it acts it becomes a whole lot easier. You can watch and listen only so much, getting stuck in a doing it is a far better way to learn. You can feel it, see it and hear it. You learn how to position yourself, how your movements and actions affect the end result.

I think the worst thing to be afraid of is not getting it right, but this is the best way to learn, make mistakes as these mistakes will show you how to improve. I've been welding for six or seven years in various industries, I still make mistakes and its how I improve.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: It's more of a quick jab than a drag to knock off the flux, watch the puddle not the spark. I still like welding but haven't done it for years now. If you get a flash with bare eyes and that night you think your eyes are full of hot sand get a couple of used tea bags and lay them on your eye lids it will take away the burn. Don't worry about the sparks unless one rolls down your shirt or pant leg lol. Cheers now you know every thing I do about welding

author: @richardsandwell2285
text:
Practice is the main thing, use scrap metal, once you get used to it, you can tell your arc length is correct by the sound.
With thin stuff little tacks like you did then join them up, often you can use a stop-start method, providing the slag does not have time to cool you can kind off put the rod in take it off and back in again. hard to describe on a comment. 
I have done welding years so it is all too easy for me to say, but you will get there Robert. 
Some horrendous welding projects I have had to do in the past during my career.
It sounds like you have an inverter welder, I had to learn on an AC machine, it at a big heavy transformer and it used to dance across the floor of the workshop its laminates were so loose. 
DC machines are better and produce nicer results.

author: @badWithComputer
text: Arc welding is most suited to thick stuff, it's the welding method of choice for big dirty farm machinery. don't be put off with "bad" looking welds on thin stuff, you are in at the deep end with arc and sheet metal.

author: @TERRYB0688
text: Robert, Stick welding can produce some nice welds, your looking for the puddling this is where both metal and rod become molten , yes it's a good idea to hold the rod to control the position of the stick, the good thing about stick welding is it is portable without bottles, keep practicing 👴🏻

author: @BensWorkshop
text:
I hope you have a hard hat... There will be people coming here to shout about your welds....

For thin sheet, you are better off with a MIG. Particularly around 1mm and below. For 2mm and above, and arc is really great. You can weld thin stuff with an arc, but you need to do much smaller welds.
